messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.
Find me a few foundational papers on the transformer architecture.
Searching arXiv for foundational transformer papers.

{"papers": [{"id": "1706.03762", "title": "Attention Is All You Need", "authors": [{"name": "Ashish Vaswani", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Noam Shazeer", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Niki Parmar", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks. We propose the Transformer, a model architecture based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments show these models are superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG"], "published": "2017-06-12T17:57:34Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "1910.10683", "title": "Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer", "authors": [{"name": "Colin Raffel", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Noam Shazeer", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Adam Roberts", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We explore transfer learning for NLP by introducing a unified framework that converts every language problem into a text-to-text format, studying the limits of transfer learning with our Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer (T5).", "categories": ["cs.LG", "cs.CL"], "published": "2019-10-23T17:37:36Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.10683", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2010.11929", "title": "An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale", "authors": [{"name": "Alexey Dosovitskiy", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Lucas Beyer", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Alexander Kolesnikov", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We show that a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks, attaining excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring fewer resources to train.", "categories": ["cs.CV", "cs.LG"], "published": "2020-10-22T17:55:01Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 4123, "start_index": 0}

Here are the foundational hits:
1. **Attention Is All You Need** (1706.03762, Vaswani et al., 2017) - introduces the Transformer, dropping recurrence/convolution for pure attention.
2. **Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning (T5)** (1910.10683, Raffel et al., 2019) - unifies NLP tasks in a text-to-text Transformer.
3. **An Image is Worth 16x16 Words (ViT)** (2010.11929, Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) - applies the Transformer to image patches.

Want me to pull the full text of any of these?

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

I don't have the id memorized - searching first so I don't guess it.

BERT obtains new state-of-the-art results on eleven natural language processing tasks.", "categories": ["cs.CL"], "published": "2018-10-11T00:50:01Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 1, "start_index": 0}
Found id 1810.04805 - now retrieving the full text with structure.

{"paper": {"id": "1810.04805", "title": "1810.04805", "authors": [], "abstract_text": "", "categories": [], "published": "", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, "source": "html", "raw_markdown": "# BERT

...", "pruned_markdown": "# BERT

...", "chunks": [{"index": 0, "start_char": 0, "end_char": 120, "text": "Context: Approach -> Pre-training BERT

BERT pretrains deep bidirectional representations using a masked language model objective plus next-sentence prediction.", "cluster_id": "Pre-training BERT", "parent_id": "Approach"}, {"index": 1, "start_char": 120, "end_char": 232, "text": "Context: Approach -> Fine-tuning BERT

Download the full text of 1412.6980 as markdown.
op=download returns the full paper as markdown text.

{"id": "1412.6980", "op": "download", "result": "# Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

## Abstract
We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization...

## 2. Algorithm
We compute biased first/second moment estimates m_t and v_t, bias-correct them, and update parameters by m_hat / (sqrt(v_hat) + epsilon)."}

Here's the full markdown for 1412.6980 (Adam). It opens with the abstract, then the algorithm: maintain exponential moving averages of the gradient (m_t) and its square (v_t), bias-correct both, and step by m_hat/(sqrt(v_hat)+epsilon). The complete text is in the tool output above.

Across the indexed reasoning papers, how does tree-of-thoughts differ from chain-of-thought?
Multi-paper question - using HDRR for document routing + scoped chunk retrieval.

function.name: hdrr
function.arguments: {"code": "{\"q\": \"tree of thoughts vs chain of thought reasoning strategy\", \"limit_docs\": 4, \"limit_chunks\": 8}"}

messages.content: {"query": "tree of thoughts vs chain of thought reasoning strategy", "routed_documents": ["2201.11903", "2305.10601"], "chunks": [{"id": "2201.11903-2", "text": "Chain-of-thought prompting elicits a single linear sequence of intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer."}, {"id": "2305.10601-4", "text": "Tree of Thoughts maintains a tree of partial solutions (thoughts) and explores it with search, enabling lookahead and backtracking rather than one linear path."}]}

HDRR routed to the two relevant papers and pulled the key chunks:

- **Chain-of-Thought** (2201.11903) produces a *single linear* sequence of reasoning steps, then answers.
- **Tree of Thoughts** (2305.10601) generalizes that into a *tree* of partial solutions explored with search, adding lookahead and backtracking instead of committing to one path.

So ToT trades extra compute for the ability to explore and recover from bad intermediate steps.

Pulling both abstracts to compare directly.

[{"id": "2005.11401", "op": "abstract", "result": {"id": "2005.11401", "title": "Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks", "authors": [{"name": "Patrick Lewis", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Ethan Perez", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Aleksandra Piktus", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), models that combine pre-trained parametric memory with non-parametric memory from a dense vector index of Wikipedia, accessed with a neural retriever, for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG"], "published": "2020-05-22T21:34:34Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}}, {"id": "1910.10683", "op": "abstract", "result": {"id": "1910.10683", "title": "Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer", "authors": [{"name": "Colin Raffel", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Noam Shazeer", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Adam Roberts", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We explore transfer learning for NLP by introducing a unified framework that converts every language problem into a text-to-text format, studying the limits of transfer learning with our Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer (T5).", "categories": ["cs.LG", "cs.CL"], "published": "2019-10-23T17:37:36Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.10683", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}}]

HDRR wasn't available (the server's embedded-db index isn't enabled in this build), so I fell back to search + abstracts:

- **RAG** (2005.11401) brings in knowledge *non-parametrically*: a neural retriever fetches passages from an external Wikipedia index at inference time and conditions generation on them.
- **T5** (1910.10683) stores everything *parametrically* - all knowledge lives in the model weights learned during text-to-text pre-training; there's no external lookup.

So the core difference is external retrieval vs. baked-in parameters. If you build the index, HDRR would do this routing in one call.

I keep hearing about 'rotary position embeddings'. Find the paper and explain the core idea from the actual text.
First, locate the RoPE paper.

{"papers": [{"id": "2104.09864", "title": "RoFormer: Enhanced Transformer with Rotary Position Embedding", "authors": [{"name": "Jianlin Su", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Yu Lu", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Shengfeng Pan", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We propose Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) to leverage positional information in transformer language models, encoding absolute position with a rotation matrix while naturally incorporating relative position dependency in self-attention.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG"], "published": "2021-04-20T08:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.09864", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 3, "start_index": 0}
Retrieving RoFormer with structure to read the method.

{"paper": {"id": "2104.09864", "title": "2104.09864", "authors": [], "abstract_text": "", "categories": [], "published": "", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.09864", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, "source": "html", "raw_markdown": "# RoFormer

## Rotary Position Embedding

...", "pruned_markdown": "# RoFormer

## Rotary Position Embedding

...", "chunks": [{"index": 0, "start_char": 0, "end_char": 118, "text": "Context: Proposed Approach -> Rotary Position Embedding

RoPE encodes absolute position by multiplying token embeddings with a rotation matrix whose angle depends on position.", "cluster_id": "Rotary Position Embedding", "parent_id": "Proposed Approach"}, {"index": 1, "start_char": 118, "end_char": 308, "text": "Context: Proposed Approach -> Properties

Because rotations compose additively, the dot product between two rotated vectors depends only on their relative offset, injecting relative-position information directly into self-attention.", "cluster_id": "Properties", "parent_id": "Proposed Approach"}], "hierarchical_chunks": null}

The paper is **RoFormer** (arXiv:2104.09864). From its method text: RoPE multiplies each token's embedding by a *rotation matrix* whose angle is a function of the token's absolute position. The trick is that rotations compose, so the attention dot product between two tokens ends up depending only on their *relative* distance. That gives you absolute-position encoding that automatically expresses relative position inside self-attention - no extra learned position table, and it extrapolates better to longer sequences.

Compare how ResNet and ViT approach image recognition.
Grabbing both abstracts in one batched call to compare.

[{"id": "1512.03385", "op": "abstract", "result": {"id": "1512.03385", "title": "Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition", "authors": [{"name": "Kaiming He", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Xiangyu Zhang", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Shaoqing Ren", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously, reformulating layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs.", "categories": ["cs.CV"], "published": "2015-12-10T19:51:55Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1512.03385", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}}, {"id": "2010.11929", "op": "abstract", "result": {"id": "2010.11929", "title": "An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale", "authors": [{"name": "Alexey Dosovitskiy", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Lucas Beyer", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Alexander Kolesnikov", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We show that a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks, attaining excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring fewer resources to train.", "categories": ["cs.CV", "cs.LG"], "published": "2020-10-22T17:55:01Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}}]

- **ResNet** (1512.03385) stays convolutional but adds *residual connections* (learning F(x)=H(x)-x), which lets you train very deep CNNs without degradation.
- **ViT** (2010.11929) drops convolution: it splits the image into patches and feeds them to a standard *Transformer* encoder, beating CNNs once pre-trained on enough data.

In short: ResNet makes depth trainable within the CNN paradigm; ViT replaces the paradigm with attention.

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.
I have three papers indexed and want to know what they collectively say about scaling laws. One call, please.
Collective/cross-paper question over the index - HDRR is the right single call (not three retrieve_paper calls).

{"query": "scaling laws model size data compute", "routed_documents": ["2005.14165", "2302.13971", "1910.10683"], "chunks": [{"id": "2005.14165-7", "text": "Performance improves smoothly and predictably as we scale parameters, data, and compute; few-shot ability strengthens with size."}, {"id": "2302.13971-3", "text": "Smaller models trained on more tokens can outperform larger models trained on fewer, motivating compute-optimal training."}]}

One HDRR call routed across all three indexed papers. Collectively on scaling:
- **GPT-3** (2005.14165): capability improves smoothly and predictably with more parameters/data/compute, and few-shot ability strengthens with scale.
- **LLaMA** (2302.13971): pushes the compute-optimal angle - a smaller model trained on *more tokens* can beat a bigger one, so token budget matters as much as parameter count.

Net: scale helps predictably, but the parameter-vs-token allocation is itself a lever.

Quick conceptual question: what's the difference between precision and recall?

Precision = TP / (TP + FP): of the items you flagged positive, how many actually are. Recall = TP / (TP + FN): of all the real positives, how many you caught. Precision punishes false alarms; recall punishes misses. You usually trade one for the other (the F1 score is their harmonic mean).

That's general ML knowledge, so no paper lookup needed - but if you want the source of a specific metric or a paper that analyzes the trade-off, I can search arXiv.
